subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given statement is p∧~q∨~p

Let us take A∩Bc∪Ac.

Now let us apply distributive law to the above statement.

≡A∪Ac∩Bc∪Ac

≡U∩Bc∪Ac

≡Bc∪Ac

Now the given statement p∧~q∨~p will be equivalent to ~q∨~p or ~p∨~q.

Hence the given statement is equivalent to ~p∨~q,

Now negation of given statement by demorgan's law will be,

~~p∨~q≡p∧q

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$p \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$ has truth value $\mathrm{F}$.
It means $p \rightarrow(\sim p \vee q)$ is false.
It means $p$ is true and $\sim p \vee q$ is false.
$\Rightarrow p$ is true and both $\sim p$ and $q$ are false.
$\Rightarrow p$ is true and $q$ is false.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
~p⇔~q∧q  =p⇔q∧q

Proof: Solving the given ~p⇔~q

=~p→~q∧~q→p

=~~p∨~q∧q∨p

=~~p∧q∧p∨q

=p∧q∨~p∨q      ...1

Now, p⇔q≡p→q∧q→p

=~p∨q∧~q∨p

=~p∨q∧~q∨~p∨q∧p

=~p∧~q∨q∧~q∨~p∧p∨q∧p

=~p∧~q∨c∨c∨q∧p where c is contradiction

=~p∧~q∨p∧q

=~p∨q∨p∧q        ...2

So from equation 1 & 2 we get

~p⇔~q∧q≡p⇔q∧q

p q p↔q p↔q∧q p→q p→q∧q p→1∧p
T T T T T T T
T F F F F F F
F T F F T T F
F F T F T F F

∴  ~p⇔~q∧q is equivalent to p⇒q∧p.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find ~p↔q→p

We know ~p↔q≡p∧~q∨q∧~p

So ~p↔q→p

≡p∧~q→p∨q→p∧~p

Now p∧~q→p≡p∧q∧~p≡p∧~p∧q≡c

and q→p∧~p≡~q∨p∧~p≡~p∧~q∨p

≡~p∧~q∨~p∧p=~p∧~q

Hence ~p↔q→p≡c∨~p∧~q≡~p∧~q

solution:
Negation of given statement is

=~~s∨~r∧s

Using De Morgan's law ~A∨B=~A∧~B

=~~s∧~~ r ∧s

Using double negation law ~~A=A, and De Morgan's law

=s∧~~r∨~s

=s∧r∨~s 

Using distributive law A∧B∨C=A∧B∨A∧C

=s∧r∨s∧~s

=s∧r∨F

=s∧r.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given $\sim(p \vee \sim q) \vee \sim(p \vee q)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\equiv(\sim p \vee q) \vee(\sim p \vee \sim q) \\
&\equiv \sim p \vee(q \vee \sim q) \\
&\equiv \sim p
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& p \rightarrow(q \rightarrow p)=\sim p \vee(q \rightarrow p) \\
& =\sim p \vee(\sim q \vee p) \quad \text { since } p \vee \sim p \text { is always true } \\
& =\sim p \vee p \vee q=p \rightarrow(p \vee q) .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
options: Statement $-1$ is false, Statement $-2$ is true
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true, Statement $-2$ is a correct explanation for Statement $-1$
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is true; Statement $-2$ is not a correct explanation for Statement $-1$.
Statement $-1$ is true, Statement $-2$ is false.
solution:
Given statement $r=\sim p \leftrightarrow q$
Statement $-1: r_1=(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge q)$
Statement $-2: r_2=\sim(p \leftrightarrow \sim q)=(p \wedge q) \vee(\sim q \wedge \sim p)$
From the truth table of $r, r_1$ and $r_2$, $r=r_1$.
Hence Statement $-1$ is true and Statement $-2$ is false.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
LHS of all the options are same i.e. ((P→Q)∧~Q)

≡(~P∨Q)∧~Q

≡(~P∧~Q)∨(Q∧~Q)

≡~P∧~Q

(A) (~P∧~Q)→Q

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨Q

≡(P∨Q)∨Q≠ tautology

(B) (~P∧~Q)→~P

≡~(~P∧~Q)∨~P

≡(P∨Q)∨~P

(C) (~P∧~Q)→P

≡(P∨Q)∨P≠ Tautology

(D) (~P∧~Q)→(P∧Q)

≡(P∨Q)∨(P∧Q)≠ Tautology
